title: Spoonbread
subtitle: A Southern American baked cornmeal pudding, soft enough to serve by the spoon
excerpt: Spoonbread is a baked cornmeal dish from the American South that sits between porridge and soufflé. Cooked polenta is enriched with butter, eggs and milk, then baked until puffed and set around the edges but still trembling at the centre. It is served by the spoon, not sliced.
sourceNotes: Spoonbread has roots in the traditional corn cookery of the American South, with documented recipes appearing in Virginia and Kentucky cookbooks from the nineteenth century. The dish is related to the Native American tradition of corn porridge and was adopted into colonial cooking. The technique of enriching cooked cornmeal with eggs and milk before baking is consistent across historical sources. This recipe is synthesised from that tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cooked polenta base can be made up to an hour ahead and kept warm. Stir in the eggs and milk just before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional or fan; the dish needs even heat to set evenly

glossaryTerms.definition: Spoonbread starts with polenta cooked into a thick porridge before the eggs and dairy are added. Cooking the polenta first gelatinises the starch, giving the final dish its characteristic texture: crisp and set around the outside, soft and almost custardy in the centre. If the base is not cooked through before baking, the spoonbread will be grainy.

content.text: Spoonbread belongs to the American South the way Yorkshire pudding belongs to England: a baked batter dish, side to a main, served warm. The key is the
. You cook the cornmeal into a thick mush first, then enrich it with butter, eggs and milk and bake until it puffs. The centre should wobble when you pull it from the oven; it sets as it rests.

A cast-iron skillet gives the best crust on the outside edges, but any ovenproof dish about 23 cm wide works. Serve straight from the dish within 10 minutes of coming out of the oven before it deflates.

Heat the oven to 190 degrees C. Butter a 23 cm cast-iron skillet or ovenproof dish and put it in the oven to heat while you make the batter. Bring the 500 ml milk and 1 tsp salt to a near-simmer in a medium saucepan over medium heat. Pour in the 120 g polenta in a thin stream, whisking constantly. Cook over low heat for 5 to 7 minutes, stirring with a wooden spoon, until the mixture thickens to the consistency of porridge and starts to pull away from the sides of the pan. Remove from the heat and beat in the 30 g butter until melted.

Allow the polenta to cool for 5 minutes. Stir in the 3 egg yolks one at a time, then stir in the 1 tsp baking powder. In a clean bowl, whisk the 3 egg whites to stiff peaks. Fold a third of the whites into the polenta to loosen it, then fold in the remaining whites in two additions, keeping as much air as possible.

Remove the hot skillet from the oven and pour in the batter. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the edges are set and golden brown, pulling away from the sides, and the centre still has a slight wobble when shaken. Leave to rest for 5 minutes before serving. Bring the dish straight to the table and serve by the spoonful.

items.problem: Spoonbread is dense and heavy rather than light
items.solution: The egg whites were not whipped to stiff peaks, or they were overmixed when folded in and lost their air. Whip until the whites hold a clean peak that does not droop, then fold gently using a large spoon in a figure-eight motion.

items.problem: Centre is still liquid after 35 minutes
items.solution: The polenta base was not cooked long enough before the eggs were added. The base must be thick and glossy from the pan, not thin. Give it another 5 minutes of baking and check again; the edges should be pulling away from the dish.

items.problem: Spoonbread deflates completely within a minute of coming out
items.solution: This is partly inevitable as the steam escapes. Serve within 10 minutes of baking. A slight settle is normal; complete collapse suggests the whites were undermixed or the oven was too cool.
